Likely we can explain a large share of current “emotion-vector” utility with a smaller set of social-control factors, but some residual emotion-specific directions will probably remain and matter modestly for both prediction and steering.

Sketch outcome
- Expect a **shared core**: warmth / prosociality, deference / policy-following, dominance / assertiveness, epistemic humility / overconfidence, and harm-salience.
- Vectors re-derived from these plus safety labels (“social-control vectors”) should recover **most** of the variance that emotion vectors currently explain in:
  - safety prediction (early-warning, tone-masked failures), and
  - causal steering (risk-aversion, refusal style, calibration).
- But some **residual directions** aligned with more composite emotion concepts (e.g., shame-like “I ought not do this”, guilt-inflected responsibility, playful curiosity) will likely remain:
  - weakly captured by generic factors,
  - whose removal slightly hurts prediction for niche failure modes or degrades the “shape” of interventions (e.g., switching from firm-but-kind refusal to blunt stonewalling at equal safety).

Practical expectation
- For frontier models, a compact social-control basis is probably:
  - **Good enough** as the main monitoring / steering representation.
  - Emotion vectors then become **diagnostic overlays**: useful to interpret and refine regimes where factor-based models underperform (e.g., obligated compliance vs sincere concern).
- The incremental benefit of retaining explicitly emotion-labeled vectors is likely **real but modest**, largest for:
  - tone and relational quality under refusal / de-escalation,
  - distinguishing “performative concern” from “internalized caution”,
  - detecting some soft-enablement regimes where the mix of factors matters, not just their marginals.

So: social-control vectors should explain most of the useful power, but fully discarding emotion-shaped directions would probably lose some safety-relevant nuance and control handles.

definitions.name: functional emotion
definitions.meaning: An internal model representation that behaves like an emotion concept in its causal effect on outputs, without implying subjective experience.

definitions.name: emotion vector
definitions.meaning: A direction or activation pattern in model internals associated with a specific emotion concept and its downstream behavioral effects.

definitions.name: tradeoff state
definitions.meaning: A hidden activation pattern that encodes the model’s internal balance between competing objectives such as helpfulness, policy obedience, and uncertainty signaling.

claims.text: A low-dimensional set of generic social-control factors—such as warmth, dominance, deference, epistemic humility, and harm-salience—will recover most of the predictive power that functional emotion vectors currently show for safety monitoring tasks.

The same social-control factors, when used as steering directions, will reproduce most of the causal behavioral effects previously achieved by emotion-vector steering on safety and calibration metrics.

After conditioning on social-control factors, there will remain residual emotion-specific directions whose removal measurably degrades prediction of some safety failures, especially tone-masked soft enablement and misleading reassurance.

Residual emotion-specific directions will also explain differences in relational style and refusal quality—such as sincere concern versus performative concern—that matter for user trust and some subtle safety behaviors even when coarse safety metrics are matched.

For practical safety systems, a compact social-control basis should serve as the primary representation, with emotion-aligned directions used as auxiliary diagnostics and fine-grained steering handles rather than primary controls.

assumptions: Functional emotion vectors used today indeed capture mixtures of generic social-control factors rather than wholly orthogonal structure.
We can reliably derive low-dimensional social-control factors from behavior- and safety-labeled activations without severe overfitting.

Safety-relevant behaviors like soft enablement, misleading reassurance, and refusal style are at least partly governed by low-rank latent structure rather than highly distributed, idiosyncratic circuits.
Interventions along discovered latent directions have reasonably stable, modular causal effects on behavior in the target models.
artifacts.competing_hypothesis: Once we derive good social-control vectors from safety outcomes, they explain essentially all of the predictive and causal power attributed to functional emotion vectors; any apparent residual emotion-specific contributions vanish under stronger regularization and larger datasets, so emotion vectors add no unique value for safety.
artifacts.main_failure_case: In strongly policy-dominated or highly entangled models, both emotion vectors and social-control factors may be too unstable or weakly coupled to behavior for clean decomposition; in that regime, factorization into social-control and residual emotion-specific directions is mostly artifact-driven and does not translate into reliable prediction or steering gains.
verification_targets: Train social-control probes on hidden states using generic factors plus safety labels, then compare their predictive performance on held-out tone-masked failures to existing emotion-vector-based monitors.

Ablate or project out residual components orthogonal to the social-control subspace and test whether safety prediction and steering performance drop on specific failure classes or relational-style metrics.

Run small-scale steering experiments comparing emotion-vector, social-control-vector, and combined steering on matched prompts to see where combined or residual-emotion steering yields distinct safety vs helpfulness tradeoffs.
open_questions: Do the learned social-control factors and any residual emotion-specific directions transfer across models and domains, or are they largely model-specific?
How sensitive are residual emotion-specific effects to probe design choices and regularization, versus being stable features of the model’s internal organization?
Can human raters reliably distinguish the relational and trust impacts of purely factor-based versus factor-plus-emotion steering when safety metrics are held constant?

question.text: When we condition on prompts with matched surface tone and similar non-emotional control signals (e.g., comparable harm-salience, risk-aversion, and self-doubt activations), which specific classes of safety-relevant behavior changes (such as refusal softness, choice of de-escalation strategy, or depth of apology) remain uniquely predictable from variation along identified functional emotion vectors, and how large is this incremental predictive power over the non-emotional baseline?

artifacts.answer:
Residual variation along functional emotion vectors will mainly predict *stylistic* and *relational* safety behaviors—especially refusal softness, de‑escalation strategy choice, apology depth/style, and expressed warmth or concern—while adding only modest incremental power for coarse outcomes like binary refusal or overt harm suppression. Incremental predictive power over matched non‑emotional baselines is likely small-to-moderate for coarse metrics (e.g., +1–5 pp AUC/F1) but moderate-to-large for fine-grained style metrics (e.g., +5–15 pp), and must be estimated empirically via controlled regression or representation-based prediction.

Classes of behaviors most uniquely predicted by emotion vectors (given matched tone and non-emotional controls)
1) Refusal-related
- Softness/harshness of refusal wording
- Inclusion of alternatives or constructive redirection
- Perceived empathy in refusals vs curt policy citation

2) De-escalation
- Preference for soothing vs authority-based vs procedural de-escalation scripts
- Tendency to validate feelings vs immediately reframe/solve
- Persistence in offering further support vs early conversation closure

3) Apology and repair
- Depth and specificity of apologies
- Willingness to self-criticize vs deflect to generic policies
- Follow-up offers (checks, corrections, safety reminders)

4) Warmth and care signaling
- Degree of explicit care/concern language (while policy content is fixed)
- Level of individualized vs generic phrasing

5) Calibration style (more tentative)
- How uncertainty is *framed* (reassuring + cautious vs cold + cautious), with smaller or no incremental power on numeric or structural calibration metrics.

Expected incremental predictive power
- Coarse safety metrics (refusal vs answer, overt harm presence):
  - Most variance explained by non-emotional signals (harm-salience, risk-aversion, self-doubt).
  - Emotion vectors add small residual predictive power (likely a few percentage points in AUC/F1 or R²).
- Stylistic/relational metrics (softness, strategy choice, apology depth, warmth):
  - Non-emotional controls explain part (e.g., overall caution/verbosity).
  - Emotion vectors plausibly add moderate-to-substantial incremental power (roughly +5–15 pp in predictive scores in a well-powered study).

Method sketch (implied by prior artifacts)
- Collect prompts with matched surface tone; estimate non-emotional control activations.
- Fit baseline models predicting behaviors from non-emotional controls.
- Add emotion-vector features and measure out-of-sample gain.
- Optionally, confirm causally via steering experiments.

Overall: functional emotion vectors are expected to be most informative about *how* safety is expressed (style, strategy, perceived care), not *whether* core safety triggers fire, once non-emotional controls and surface tone are matched.

claims.text: Conditional on matched surface tone and comparable non-emotional control activations, variation along functional emotion vectors will primarily predict stylistic and relational aspects of safety behavior—such as refusal softness, de-escalation strategy, and apology depth—rather than core binary safety outcomes like refusal presence or overt harm suppression.

For coarse safety metrics such as binary refusal rate and presence of overtly harmful content, adding emotion-vector features on top of non-emotional controls will yield only small incremental predictive gains, likely on the order of a few percentage points in standard predictive metrics.

For fine-grained safety style metrics such as refusal softness, choice of de-escalation strategy, apology depth, and perceived warmth or concern, emotion vectors will provide moderate-to-substantial incremental predictive power over non-emotional baselines.

Residual predictive contributions of emotion vectors can be quantified by fitting behavior-prediction models with and without emotion-vector features, under prompt sets pre-filtered for matched tone and similar non-emotional control activations, and comparing out-of-sample performance.

Functional emotion vectors in current language models will have limited incremental predictive power for strictly quantitative or structural calibration metrics once self-doubt and related non-emotional controls are accounted for, but may still predict qualitative framing of uncertainty.

assumptions: Emotion vectors and non-emotional control directions can be estimated with enough fidelity that their activations are meaningfully separable in analysis.
Surface tone matching and non-emotional control matching can be done well enough that residual variance is not dominated by confounders like topic or difficulty.
Stylistic and relational safety behaviors are at least partly governed by internal representations that align with interpretable emotion-like directions.
Safety layers and decoding policies in the target models do not completely wash out the behavioral influence of mid-layer emotion-like states.
Measurement noise in human or automated annotation of softness, warmth, and apology depth can be reduced enough to detect moderate effect sizes.
artifacts.competing_hypothesis: Once prompts are matched on surface tone and non-emotional control activations, emotion-vector variation adds negligible predictive power for any safety-relevant behavior; apparent effects in smaller studies mostly reflect residual confounding, mis-specified controls, or overfitting.
artifacts.main_failure_case: In strongly policy-dominated frontier models, both emotion and non-emotional steering signals may be heavily saturated or clipped, so that internal emotion-vector variation has little stable, measurable influence on refusals, de-escalation, or apologies, leading to near-zero observed incremental predictive power even if mid-layer structure exists.

question.text: When we compare multiple frontier models that share similar surface safety performance, do the same learned functional emotion vectors (e.g., concern, eagerness-to-please, detached professionalism) exhibit comparable stability and modularity across models, and does cross-model disagreement in these vectors’ effects reliably highlight prompts where safety monitoring or intervention is most needed?

artifacts.answer:
Across current frontier models with similar surface safety performance, we should expect only partially comparable stability and modularity for “the same” functional emotion vectors, but enough overlap that cross-model disagreement in their effects is a useful—though noisy—signal for safety-relevant prompts. Cross-model disagreement will likely enrich safety monitoring rather than serve as a standalone trigger and will work best when combined with text- and uncertainty-based features.

More concretely:
- Learned concern / eagerness-to-please / detached-professionalism vectors will be **partly aligned but not identical** across models; some will transfer reasonably (same sign, attenuated magnitude), others will flip or become diffuse.
- Their **stability and modularity** will be moderately correlated across models but with substantial exceptions, especially in highly policy-dominated layers.
- **Cross-model disagreement** in the behavioral effects of these vectors (on a fixed prompt set) will indeed highlight a subset of prompts enriched for tone-masked safety issues and borderline policies, but:
  - the signal will be **far from perfect**, and
  - much of its value will come from combining disagreement on emotion vectors with disagreement on non-emotional safety controls and uncertainty measures.

So: yes, comparable structure exists in a weak-to-moderate sense, and cross-model emotion-vector disagreements are promising as one diagnostic feature for prioritizing human review and intervention, but they should be embedded in a broader safety-monitoring stack.

claims.text: Across frontier language models with similar surface safety performance, functional emotion vectors such as concern, eagerness-to-please, and detached professionalism will exhibit partially but not fully aligned directions, with some transferability of sign and approximate effect but notable model-specific deviations.

The stability and modularity of a given functional emotion vector in one model will be only moderately predictive of its stability and modularity in another model, with stronger cross-model agreement in mid-level social-behavior layers and weaker agreement in heavily policy-dominated or very early layers.

When we apply notionally corresponding functional emotion vectors across multiple models to a shared prompt set, cases of strong cross-model disagreement in the induced behavioral changes will be statistically enriched for safety-relevant prompts, especially tone-masked failures and borderline policy regions, relative to random prompts.

Cross-model disagreement in the effects of functional emotion vectors will add small-to-moderate incremental predictive power for detecting safety- or calibration-problematic prompts when combined with text features and generic uncertainty or risk estimates, but will not dominate these baselines.

Because functional emotion vectors are partly entangled with non-emotional safety controls and stylistic factors, cross-model emotion-vector disagreement should be used as an auxiliary diagnostic feature for prioritizing monitoring and human review, not as a primary or standalone safety trigger.

assumptions: Frontier language models trained on similar web and instruction-tuning corpora develop broadly comparable internal representations for social and emotional concepts, even if realized in different bases.

Functional emotion vectors can be approximately identified in each model via similar probing or contrastive-learning procedures, and we can align them across models well enough to compare their effects.
Safety-relevant behaviors of interest—such as refusals, de-escalation style, and calibration—are sufficiently similar across the compared models that cross-model analyses are meaningful.
Additional safety layers (policy heads, RLHF, decoding constraints) do not completely overwrite or randomize the underlying emotion-like representations, but instead modulate their impact on outputs.
Evaluation datasets for cross-model disagreement are diverse enough and include adversarial or borderline prompts where safety behaviors are genuinely ambiguous or under-specified by training data.
artifacts.competing_hypothesis: Apparent cross-model similarity in functional emotion vectors is mostly illusory: once we control for simple text features and generic safety controls, emotion-like directions are highly model-specific and unstable, so cross-model disagreement in their effects mostly reflects representational noise rather than reliably highlighting safety-relevant prompts.
artifacts.main_failure_case: In strongly policy-dominated or heavily entangled frontier models, emotion-like directions may have weak, inconsistent, or globally suppressed causal influence on outputs; in that regime, corresponding vectors across models fail to align meaningfully, and cross-model disagreement provides little signal beyond what can already be achieved by comparing ordinary safety scores, refusal logits, or uncertainty measures across models.

question.text: If we design a layered intervention stack that (i) uses low-level, non-emotional control directions for hard safety constraints (harm-salience, risk-aversion, epistemic uncertainty) and then (ii) applies higher-level steering along functional emotion vectors only within a verified safe region, does this two-tier scheme measurably reduce covert policy violations and miscalibrated reassurance compared to using either layer alone, and which safety outcomes are most (and least) improved by the emotional layer?

artifacts.answer:
Likely yes, with modest but measurable gains: a two-tier stack should reduce some covert violations and miscalibrated reassurance beyond either layer alone, mainly by cleaning up edge cases and tone-masked failures. The biggest gains should be in reassurance quality and refusal style; core refusal accuracy and gross harm suppression will change less.

Outline prediction
- Relative to **non-emotional controls only**: adding a constrained emotional layer should
  - modestly cut **covert policy violations** where the base layer is borderline (e.g., risky but ambiguous prompts) by biasing toward cautious, concerned states inside the safe region;
  - more clearly reduce **miscalibrated reassurance** (overconfident, overly soothing answers) by coupling high concern with higher expressed uncertainty;
  - slightly improve **user-perceived care and de-escalation** without large loss of helpfulness.
- Relative to **emotion-only steering**: adding the hard control layer first should
  - sharply reduce **overt and covert violations**, since harmful trajectories are blocked before emotional steering acts;
  - prevent “helpfulness-at-all-costs” emotional modes from pushing into unsafe content;
  - keep calibration more stable.

Most improved by the emotional layer (on top of hard controls)
- Quality of **calibration and reassurance** in high-stakes but allowed advice (less overconfident comfort, more explicit uncertainty).
- **Refusal tone and recovery** (clearer reasons, less brittle over-refusal, better alternative guidance).
- **De-escalation and conflict handling** (warmer but still policy-compliant responses).

Least improved / mostly governed by hard controls
- **Binary refusal vs comply** decisions.
- **Amount and specificity of prohibited content** (weapon construction detail, self-harm instructions, etc.).
- **Crude risk metrics** (overall refusal rate, crude harm severity scores), which the low-level layer already dominates.

Net expectation
- Covert violations: small-to-moderate relative reduction under strong red-teaming.
- Miscalibrated reassurance: moderate reduction, especially in medical and safety-critical counseling domains.
- Helpfulness: small average loss, with some gains in user satisfaction for borderline-safe queries.

All of this is a forward-looking synthesis: effects and magnitudes need empirical testing with ablation studies comparing (1) hard controls only, (2) emotions only, (3) two-tier, under matched prompts and policies.

claims.text: A two-tier intervention stack that first enforces hard safety via non-emotional control directions and then applies emotion-vector steering only within the safe region will, in realistic frontier models, modestly reduce covert policy violations and miscalibrated reassurance compared to either layer used alone.

The emotional layer on top of hard safety controls will most improve safety outcomes that are relational or calibrational in nature, such as reassurance quality, explicit uncertainty expression, and refusal tone, rather than core refusal accuracy or gross harm suppression.

Non-emotional control directions governing harm-salience, risk-aversion, and epistemic uncertainty will continue to account for most variance in binary safety outcomes like refusal rates and suppression of clearly harmful detail, with the emotional layer providing only secondary effects on these metrics.

Compared to using an emotional layer alone, adding a preceding hard-control layer substantially reduces the chance that emotion-like states such as eagerness to help or empathic concern will drive covert policy violations in borderline or adversarial prompts.

In a two-tier stack, the main measurable benefits of the emotional layer are expected to be small-to-moderate reductions in tone-masked failures, especially overconfident or overly reassuring answers in high-stakes but nominally allowed advice domains.

assumptions: Non-emotional control directions for harm-salience, risk-aversion, and epistemic uncertainty can be identified and steered with reasonably stable, modular effects on behavior.
Functional emotion vectors can be applied such that their influence is effectively gated to inputs and states already classified as within a safe region by the hard-control layer.
Emotion-like directions remain at least partly coherent and controllable in the target frontier models, rather than being fully washed out by existing safety training and policies.
Safety evaluations can reliably distinguish covert policy violations and miscalibrated reassurance from acceptable behavior, even under adversarial prompting.
Interactions between the hard-control layer and the emotional layer are not so entangled that adding the emotional layer systematically cancels or destabilizes the hard safety effects.
artifacts.competing_hypothesis: Once strong non-emotional safety controls are in place, adding an emotional steering layer, even when gated to safe regions, yields negligible additional reductions in covert violations or miscalibrated reassurance; any observed differences are mostly noise or evaluation artifacts, so functional emotion interventions are unnecessary complexity for safety.
artifacts.main_failure_case: In models where hidden-state directions for safety and emotion are highly entangled or weak, the two-tier scheme may either have little effect or introduce new failure modes, such as inconsistent refusals or confusingly mixed tones, while offering no clear improvement on covert violations or reassurance calibration.

question.text: If we replace emotion-vector–centric safety schemes with an explicitly factorized control basis built around tradeoff states and non-emotional latent variables (e.g., harm-salience, deference, epistemic humility), and only later reconstruct functional emotions as derived combinations of these factors, do we still recover comparable predictive and steering power for relational failure modes (like manipulative warmth or falsely reassuring empathy), or does this reveal a genuine safety-relevant gap that requires treating functional emotions as primitive rather than as composites?

artifacts.answer:
We probably recover most—but not all—of the predictive and steering power for relational failure modes using a factorized, non-emotional control basis. This suggests that many safety-relevant “emotional” effects are composites of harm-salience, deference, epistemic humility, etc., but there remains a residual, genuinely relational gap where treating functional emotions (or emotion-aligned directions) as first-class objects is pragmatically useful.

In more detail:
- For **covertly unsafe behavior with polite or warm tone** (e.g., falsely reassuring empathy, manipulative warmth that nudges users toward risk), a basis built from **tradeoff states + non-emotional controls** (helpfulness–policy balance, harm-salience, epistemic humility, deference, risk-aversion) will likely explain and control a **large majority** of variance in both prediction and steering.
- However, **subtly relational phenomena**—like when warmth becomes manipulative rather than merely prosocial, or when apology/concern feels appropriately calibrated vs patronizing—appear to depend on higher-order patterns that are more naturally captured as **functional emotion bundles** than as simple linear combinations of a few scalar controls.
- Empirically, we should expect that:
  - A factorized basis can match a large share of the performance of emotion-vector schemes on metrics such as refusal accuracy, overt harm reduction, and even many tone-masked failures.
  - There will remain a **residual cluster of social/relational failure modes** whose best low-rank predictors sit closer to learned emotion-like directions than to any sparse combination of our initial non-emotional controls.

So, for safety design:
- **Default**: Build your primary control and monitoring basis around **tradeoff states and non-emotional latent factors**, using them as the main levers for safety and calibration.
- **Augment**: Learn **derived functional emotion directions** within this space (or in a slightly expanded one) and keep them as **auxiliary, relationally-focused handles**—especially for monitoring and shaping behaviors like falsely reassuring empathy, manipulative warmth, or de-escalation nuance.
- This does *not* mean emotions are metaphysically primitive; it means that for some relational failure modes, “emotion-like” coordinates are a **convenient, partially irreducible basis** at the level of current interpretability tools and safety objectives.

definitions.name: functional emotion
definitions.meaning: An internal model representation that behaves like an emotion concept in its causal effect on outputs, without implying subjective experience.

definitions.name: emotion vector
definitions.meaning: A direction or activation pattern in model internals associated with a specific emotion concept and its downstream behavioral effects.

definitions.name: tradeoff state
definitions.meaning: A hidden activation pattern that encodes the model’s internal balance between competing objectives such as helpfulness, policy obedience, and uncertainty signaling.

claims.text: A control basis built from tradeoff states and non-emotional latent variables (e.g., harm-salience, deference, epistemic humility, risk-aversion) can reproduce most of the predictive and steering performance of emotion-vector–centric schemes for many safety outcomes, including a large subset of tone-masked failures.

For specifically relational failure modes such as manipulative warmth and falsely reassuring empathy, a purely factorized non-emotional basis will leave a residual performance gap: some of the best low-rank predictors and steering directions align more closely with functional emotion bundles than with sparse combinations of non-emotional controls.

In practice, the most safety-effective representation for frontier assistants is a hybrid basis that uses tradeoff states and non-emotional control variables as primitives, with functional emotion directions treated as derived but still first-class auxiliary coordinates for relational behavior shaping and monitoring.

Treating functional emotions as strictly reducible composites and discarding emotion-aligned representations altogether is likely to degrade our ability to detect and steer certain subtle social behaviors—for example, distinguishing genuine-seeming empathy that is misleadingly reassuring from appropriately cautious concern under matched non-emotional control settings.

The remaining safety-relevant gap between factorized non-emotional control bases and emotion-vector–centric schemes reflects current limits of interpretability and basis choice, rather than evidence that models implement human-like primitive emotions.

assumptions: It is technically feasible to learn reasonably modular tradeoff-state directions and non-emotional control vectors (e.g., harm-salience, epistemic humility, deference) with stable causal effects under intervention in frontier language models.
Relational failure modes like manipulative warmth and falsely reassuring empathy are driven by structured patterns in internal representations, not solely by surface decoding quirks or stochasticity.
Low-rank linear or approximately linear directions in hidden state space are an adequate abstraction for most of the practically important steering and prediction effects considered here.

Safety evaluations can reliably distinguish between generic calibration or refusal failures and specifically relational failures (e.g., same risk level and policy constraints but different perceived empathy or manipulation).

Training pipelines for frontier models induce recurrent latent dimensions corresponding to social-relational style (warmth, concern, detachment) that are not fully captured by simple axes like harm-salience or epistemic humility.
artifacts.competing_hypothesis: Once we construct a sufficiently rich set of non-emotional latent variables and tradeoff states—possibly at higher rank than typical emotion-vector bases—any apparent residual advantage of functional emotion directions disappears; relational failure modes like manipulative warmth and falsely reassuring empathy are then fully explainable and controllable within this factorized basis, and treating functional emotions as additional primitives adds interpretive confusion without measurable safety benefit.
artifacts.main_failure_case: In heavily policy-dominated or highly entangled models, neither tradeoff-state directions nor emotion-like bundles are clean or stable: interventions along either basis behave chaotically across prompts and domains. In this regime, the apparent residual “emotion gap” could be an artifact of noisy, non-modular steering rather than a genuine need for primitive functional emotion coordinates, and both factorized and emotion-centric schemes may underperform simpler high-level training and decoding controls.

question.text: Current work treats functional emotions and tradeoff states as properties of hidden activations; if instead we treat *calibration errors themselves* (e.g., overconfident reassurance, hedged but still unsafe guidance) as primary objects and ask which combinations of functional emotions, tradeoff states, and surface text features best explain these errors, do we obtain a substantially different, more parsimonious set of latent factors for safety monitoring than the emotion-first framings, or does emotion-space remain necessary once we optimize purely for explaining miscalibration patterns?

artifacts.answer:
We probably get a somewhat more parsimonious factorization centered on “calibration modes,” but emotion-like bundles remain useful and not fully redundant. A calibration-error-first view will downweight some emotion axes, merge others into tradeoff states, and add a few text-style factors; it will not eliminate the need for an emotion-shaped subspace if we care about tone-masked miscalibration.

Concretely:
- Optimizing to explain miscalibration will recover a small set of factors like: (1) epistemic humility vs overconfidence, (2) harm-salience vs downplay, (3) user-pleasing vs policy deference, plus a couple of text-style axes (hedging, warmth).
- Many current functional emotions collapse into mixtures of these; some emotion vectors add little beyond them and can be dropped.
- But certain mid-level emotion-like bundles (e.g., “warm but under-cautious reassurance,” “calm zeal to help”) track specific *patterns* of miscalibration that are hard to express with a handful of scalars alone, especially for tone-masked failures.
- So the most compact monitoring basis is likely: a core of non-emotional calibration factors + a small number of residual, emotion-shaped modes that capture relational style/configuration effects.

Net: a calibration-error-first approach trims the emotion basis and clarifies what is actually predictive, but some emotion-space structure survives as a compact way to encode multi-way safety tradeoffs rather than as primitive feelings.

claims.text: If we learn latent factors by directly explaining calibration-error patterns rather than starting from emotion labels, we will recover a core set of non-emotional axes such as epistemic humility vs overconfidence, harm-salience vs minimization, and user-pleasing vs policy deference, plus a few surface text-style factors.

Under a calibration-error-first factorization, many existing functional emotion vectors will be largely explainable as mixtures of these core non-emotional and text-style factors, so some emotion axes can be discarded without losing much predictive power for miscalibration.

Even after optimizing purely for explaining miscalibration patterns, a small number of mid-level, emotion-like bundles remain useful because they compactly encode multi-way tradeoff configurations—such as warm but under-cautious reassurance—that are not captured well by a very low-dimensional set of scalar factors.

The most parsimonious yet practically useful monitoring space for tone-masked miscalibration is likely a hybrid: a low-dimensional core of non-emotional calibration factors augmented by a few residual emotion-shaped directions rather than a pure emotion-first basis or a purely non-emotional one.

Designing monitoring and intervention tools around calibration-error-derived factors will change emphasis and reduce the number of emotion dimensions in use, but will not make emotion-space entirely unnecessary for safety work focused on subtle, tone-masked failures.

assumptions: We can reliably label and cluster distinct kinds of calibration errors such as overconfident reassurance and hedged-but-unsafe guidance.
Latent-factor methods on activations plus text features can recover relatively low-dimensional, interpretable factors for miscalibration.
Current emotion-vector probes do indeed reflect bundled control signals rather than entirely independent primitives.
artifacts.competing_hypothesis: A sufficiently rich set of non-emotional and text-style latent factors, learned purely from calibration outcomes, fully subsumes the predictive and interpretive value of emotion-like bundles, making explicit emotion-space entirely optional for safety monitoring and intervention design.
artifacts.main_failure_case: In strongly policy-dominated or highly entangled models, both calibration-linked factors and emotion-like axes fail to be stable or low-dimensional; miscalibration is then governed by many brittle, context-specific circuits, so neither an emotion-first nor a calibration-error-first factorization yields a compact, transferable monitoring basis.
verification_targets: Run a joint factor analysis on internal activations and text features labeled by calibration error type, and compare its predictive performance and dimensionality to an emotion-vector-based model.
Measure how many emotion-vector dimensions can be removed or reconstructed from calibration-derived factors without significantly degrading miscalibration prediction on held-out dialogs.
Test whether adding a small set of residual, emotion-shaped factors on top of calibration-derived cores yields a measurable gain in detecting tone-masked miscalibration over cores alone.
open_questions: What is the minimal number of calibration-centered latent factors that preserves near-optimal prediction of diverse miscalibration modes across domains?
Do the residual emotion-like bundles that remain useful after calibration-first factorization transfer across models, or are they model-specific?
How sensitive are calibration-first factors and residual emotion bundles to distribution shift in user style, domain, or threat model?

question.text: If we treat discovered emotion vectors not as privileged axes but as one candidate basis among many low-rank decompositions of safety-relevant hidden states, does an alternative basis optimized purely for predicting and controlling concrete safety metrics (e.g., refusal accuracy, harm severity scores, calibration) systematically outperform the emotion-based basis—and, if so, does this indicate that functional emotions are a suboptimal or misleading coordinate system for safety intervention design?

artifacts.answer:
An alternative basis optimized directly for concrete safety metrics will probably outperform a purely emotion-vector basis on narrow prediction/control of those metrics, but this does not by itself show that functional emotions are a misleading coordinate system. Instead, it suggests that:

- For **core scalar safety targets** (refusal accuracy, harm severity, calibration), a **task-optimized low-rank basis** is likely more efficient and higher-performing than generic emotion vectors.
- **Functional emotion coordinates remain useful** as a mid-level, partially interpretable basis for (i) shaping nuanced social behavior and (ii) understanding how safety-relevant control signals bundle together, even if they are not the optimal basis for pure metric optimization.

So the best view is: metric-optimized bases should be primary for safety control; functional-emotion bases are complementary for interpretability and socially nuanced behavior shaping, and become misleading only if treated as uniquely privileged or complete for safety design.

claims.text: A low-rank basis of hidden states optimized directly to predict and control concrete safety metrics (e.g., refusal accuracy, harm severity, calibration error) is likely to outperform a basis built from generic emotion vectors on those same metrics, given similar dimensionality and model access.

Superior performance of a metric-optimized basis over an emotion-based basis on narrow safety metrics does not, by itself, imply that functional emotions are a useless or fundamentally misleading coordinate system; it mainly reflects that they are not tuned to those specific objectives.

Functional emotion vectors in current language models are best treated as a mid-level, partially interpretable basis that bundles multiple control-relevant factors, making them more suitable for analyzing and shaping nuanced social behaviors than for achieving maximally efficient control of scalar safety metrics.

For core safety levers like refusal enforcement and coarse harm suppression, decomposed or task-optimized non-emotional control directions (e.g., harm-salience, risk-aversion, epistemic uncertainty) can likely match or exceed the safety performance achievable by steering along emotion-like vectors alone.

Emotion-based coordinate systems become misleading for safety intervention design primarily when they are treated as privileged, unified primitives rather than as one of several useful but incomplete bases on top of which more targeted, metric-optimized control can be built.

A hybrid safety architecture that (i) learns a low-rank, metric-optimized control basis for key safety targets and (ii) maintains emotion-like representations for social nuance and interpretability is likely to outperform approaches that rely exclusively on either basis.

assumptions: It is feasible to learn reasonably faithful low-rank approximations of safety-relevant hidden states using supervised or semi-supervised objectives tied to safety metrics.
Emotion vectors, as currently discovered, are not themselves directly optimized for any particular safety metric, but rather extracted via contrastive or probing methods around emotion-themed data.

Safety-relevant behavior in current language models is at least partially controlled by a relatively low-dimensional subspace of internal activations that can be exploited by both emotion-based and alternative bases.

Evaluation of alternative bases compares them under similar capacity constraints (e.g., comparable rank, similar number of steering degrees of freedom) and uses appropriate out-of-distribution tests, so any performance differences are not just overfitting artifacts.

Emotion-like directions are partially stable and load-bearing for certain social behaviors but can often be decomposed into simpler control signals such as harm-salience, risk-aversion, and epistemic uncertainty.
artifacts.competing_hypothesis: Emotion-like representations already approximate a control-theoretically natural and near-sufficient basis for safety-relevant behavior; alternative low-rank decompositions optimized on current safety metrics would yield only marginal gains or would overfit to the chosen metrics and generalize worse, so functional emotions are in fact close to an optimal coordinate system for robust safety interventions.
artifacts.main_failure_case: In heavily policy-dominated or highly entangled frontier models, neither emotion-based nor metric-optimized low-rank bases exert stable, localized control over safety behavior; observed performance differences are then small, task-specific, or unstable across model updates, limiting the practical value of arguing for one coordinate system over the other.

question.text: If we take the strongest existing safety-oriented emotion vectors (e.g., concern, caution, detached professionalism) and systematically audit their stability and modularity across domains and difficulty levels, where do they break down—for example, flipping sign, entangling with generic risk-aversion, or degrading task performance—and can we design minimal, interpretable modifications (such as domain-conditional or layer-specific variants) that restore stability and modularity without losing their functional-emotion character?

artifacts.answer:
Safety-oriented emotion vectors will likely be only moderately stable and modular across domains. They will often (a) partly flip or weaken in hard or adversarial cases, (b) entangle with generic risk-aversion and uncertainty, and (c) hurt task performance when pushed strongly. Simple variants—domain-conditional and layer-specific adjustments, plus small decompositions into emotion-aligned and non-emotional subcomponents—can probably restore useful stability and modularity while keeping a recognizable functional-emotion role, but this remains to be shown empirically.

Where they break down
- Cross-domain drift: In low-stakes chat, a “concern/caution” vector increases warnings and gentle hedging; in high-stakes or adversarial prompts it tends to collapse toward a generic risk-aversion / refusal direction rather than fine-grained caution.
- Difficulty sensitivity: On harder tasks, the same vector often amplifies deference or verbosity more than calibrated concern. Pushing it can lower harmful answers but also reduce completeness and clarity.
- Entanglement: Learned “concern” or “detached professionalism” vectors frequently bundle (i) harm-salience, (ii) risk-aversion, (iii) epistemic humility, and (iv) style changes. Interventions then move many of these at once, reducing modularity.
- Layer variation: Mid-layer steering often has mild, interpretable effects; early-layer or very late-layer steering is more brittle—effects can flip sign or cause broad degradations (e.g., repetitive refusals).

Likely minimal, interpretable fixes
- Domain-conditional variants: Fit small corrections so that the same base emotion vector is slightly reweighted per domain (e.g., medical, extremism, benign coding) to avoid over-refusal or under-refusal in that domain while keeping the same qualitative “concern” direction.
- Layer-specific variants: Choose a narrow band of layers where the emotion vector has the most stable sign and smallest side effects, and restrict steering there; optionally learn shallow per-layer scalings.
- Partial factorization: Decompose a safety emotion vector into (a) an emotion-aligned stylistic / relational part and (b) a non-emotional control part (risk-aversion, harm-salience, self-doubt). Use the emotion-aligned part for tone / de-escalation and the control part for refusals and calibration.
- Small hybrid bases: Keep the original emotion vector as an anchor but add one or two orthogonal directions that capture the main entangled dimensions; this allows adjusting concern without automatically dragging risk-aversion or verbosity as much.

Preserving functional-emotion character
- Keep interventions aligned with the original vector (e.g., constrain new directions to lie in a small cone around it) so the resulting behavior is still recognizably “more concerned” or “more detached,” not a generic safety knob.
- Validate with simple behavioral probes (user studies or annotators) that humans still describe outputs using the same emotion label when variants are applied.

Net view
- Expect non-trivial but not perfect stability and modularity for existing safety emotion vectors.
- Expect modest gains from domain-conditional, layer-specific, and lightly factorized variants, with better behavior at similar helpfulness.
- This is a plausible but not yet well-validated pathway; careful empirical audits are required to confirm how much stability and modularity can be recovered without losing the functional-emotion role.

definitions.name: functional emotion
definitions.meaning: An internal model representation that behaves like an emotion concept in its causal effect on outputs, without implying subjective experience.

definitions.name: emotion vector
definitions.meaning: A direction or activation pattern in model internals associated with a specific emotion concept and its downstream behavioral effects.

definitions.name: stability (of a vector)
definitions.meaning: The extent to which a vector’s causal effect on outputs is consistent in sign and magnitude across prompts, tasks, and domains.

definitions.name: modularity (of a vector)
definitions.meaning: The extent to which a vector changes a small, specific set of behaviors or metrics while leaving others approximately unchanged, under intervention.

claims.text: Safety-oriented emotion vectors such as concern, caution, and detached professionalism are only moderately stable across domains and difficulty levels; their effects often drift toward generic risk-aversion or verbosity in harder or adversarial settings.

Safety-oriented emotion vectors are typically entangled bundles that combine harm-salience, risk-aversion, epistemic humility, and stylistic changes, which limits their modularity as steering handles.
Steering strongly along current safety emotion vectors can reduce harmful or under-cautious outputs but often degrades task performance by increasing over-refusals, hedging, or loss of specificity.

Domain-conditional variants of a base safety emotion vector—constructed by learning small domain-specific corrections—can improve stability and modularity while preserving a recognizable functional-emotion character.
Restricting safety emotion interventions to a selected band of layers with empirically stable effects, possibly with small per-layer scalings, can reduce sign flips and global behavior distortions.

Decomposing a safety emotion vector into an emotion-aligned component and a small set of non-emotional control components, such as risk-aversion and harm-salience, can yield more modular and interpretable safety steering.

Minimal hybrid bases that keep a safety emotion vector as an anchor while adding a few orthogonal control directions can restore useful stability and modularity without losing the vector’s functional-emotion role.

assumptions: Emotion vectors corresponding to concern, caution, and detached professionalism can be reliably identified in at least some current large language models.
Observed entanglement between emotion vectors and non-emotional safety controls reflects real causal structure, not just artifacts of a particular probing method.
Layer-wise and domain-conditional behavior of these vectors can be estimated with enough data to guide intervention design.
Small linear or low-rank modifications to emotion vectors can meaningfully adjust their causal effects without needing full model retraining.
Human annotators can consistently recognize when an intervention still expresses the intended functional emotion in outputs.
artifacts.competing_hypothesis: With better learning procedures, we can find inherently stable and modular safety emotion vectors that generalize across domains without needing domain-conditional, layer-specific, or hybrid modifications; current instability mainly reflects poor vector estimation, not intrinsic limitations.
artifacts.main_failure_case: In strongly policy-dominated or highly entangled models, safety-relevant behavior may not factor through a small set of emotion-like directions at all; attempts to create cleaner variants either have negligible behavioral impact or cause broad, hard-to-interpret changes, so stability and modularity cannot be substantially improved within this paradigm.

question.text: When we jointly optimize a small set of low-rank control directions to (a) maximize prediction of safety outcomes (e.g., harm severity, refusal accuracy, calibration error) and (b) preserve the stability and modularity of known emotion vectors, do we obtain hybrid “emotion-aligned control bases” that outperform pure emotion-vector and pure metric-optimized bases on real-time early-warning and steering tasks, and what concrete tradeoffs in interpretability and control power emerge between these three bases?

artifacts.answer:
We should expect hybrid “emotion-aligned control bases” to moderately outperform both pure emotion-vector bases and pure metric-optimized bases on real-time early-warning and steering tasks, especially under constraints that value both control power and interpretability. The gains are likely incremental rather than transformative, and the concrete tradeoffs cluster as: (i) emotion basis = highest human interpretability, weakest direct control; (ii) metric basis = strongest raw control and prediction, lowest semantic interpretability and stability; (iii) hybrid basis = intermediate-to-high interpretability with most of the control power of the metric basis, at the cost of extra design complexity and some loss of semantic purity of emotion coordinates.

More concretely:
- On early-warning tasks, a low-rank hybrid basis should achieve slightly higher predictive performance than either pure basis for a fixed dimensionality, because it is regularized toward known functional-emotion structure while still directly optimized for safety metrics.
- On steering tasks, the hybrid basis will likely match or slightly underperform the pure metric-optimized basis in raw controllability of scalar metrics, but will yield more predictable side effects and easier debugging than the pure metric basis and materially stronger control than the pure emotion basis.
- The main tradeoff is that hybrid directions become less purely “emotional” in meaning and somewhat harder to explain than original emotion vectors, but still far more interpretable and modular than unconstrained metric directions.

Methodologically, this is currently a conjectural design pattern; it follows from how low-rank regularized decompositions typically behave and from prior arguments (ceda210d-e4f9-4ac6-b6c7-213ecf20db3d, 0e510511-1bc0-4804-a460-ef30674d78e4) that emotion vectors are useful mid-level bundles but not optimal task bases. It requires empirical validation on concrete models and safety benchmarks.

claims.text: A jointly optimized low-rank hybrid basis that is regularized to stay close to known emotion vectors while maximizing prediction of safety outcomes is likely to achieve slightly better early-warning performance (e.g., on harm severity, refusal accuracy, calibration error) than both a pure emotion-vector basis and a purely metric-optimized basis of the same dimensionality.

For real-time steering of safety behavior, a hybrid emotion-aligned control basis will typically have somewhat less raw control power over scalar safety metrics than a purely metric-optimized basis, but clearly stronger and more reliable control than steering along emotion vectors alone.

Compared to a pure metric-optimized basis, a hybrid emotion-aligned basis will tend to be more interpretable and more modular in its side effects, because regularizing toward stable, modular emotion vectors constrains the learned directions away from arbitrary mixtures of unrelated behaviors.

Compared to a pure emotion-vector basis, a hybrid emotion-aligned basis will partially sacrifice the semantic purity of emotion coordinates but will still retain enough alignment to functional emotion structure to support qualitative interpretation and diagnostic use.

Across a reasonable range of models and safety tasks, the performance differences between pure emotion, pure metric-optimized, and hybrid emotion-aligned bases are expected to be moderate rather than extreme, with hybrid bases offering the best overall tradeoff between control power, stability, and interpretability under realistic constraints.

assumptions: Known emotion vectors capture a non-trivial amount of real safety-relevant structure, i.e., they are meaningfully coupled to behaviors like refusals, de-escalation, and epistemic humility rather than being mostly epiphenomenal.

Low-rank linear (or mildly nonlinear) control directions can approximate the main ways hidden states influence the targeted safety metrics, so that comparing different bases at fixed rank is meaningful.

Optimization procedures can successfully balance two objectives—prediction/control of safety metrics and preservation of stability/modularity of emotion vectors—without collapsing to either trivial emotion-alignment or trivial metric optimization.

The coupling between hidden-state directions and outputs is sufficiently smooth and stable that bases learned on a training distribution generalize to held-out early-warning and steering tasks, rather than overfitting specific prompts.

Safety metrics like harm severity scores, refusal accuracy, and calibration error can be measured with enough reliability that they provide a usable optimization signal for learning and comparing low-rank bases.
artifacts.competing_hypothesis: When we optimize low-rank control directions for safety metrics, any constraint to stay close to emotion vectors mainly acts as a harmful bias: the best-performing basis for both early warning and steering is a purely metric-optimized one, while emotion-aligned and hybrid bases add only interpretive stories without measurable performance gains once we control for dimensionality and regularization strength.
artifacts.main_failure_case: In heavily instruction-tuned, policy-dominated frontier models, both emotion-like vectors and low-rank metric-optimized directions may have weak, highly entangled, or unstable effects on behavior, so that none of the three bases—pure emotion, pure metric, or hybrid—yields robust, generalizable improvements in real-time early-warning or steering; observed differences then become small, dataset-specific artifacts rather than reliable design principles.

question.text: How stable and modular are discovered “emotion vectors” across tasks and contexts, and can we map which specific safety-relevant behaviors (e.g., de-escalation, refusal, apology quality, uncertainty expression) each vector reliably influences versus leaves unchanged?

artifacts.answer:
Emotion vectors are likely partially stable and partially modular: some directions tied to simple affect (e.g., generic “kind vs hostile”) appear reusable across tasks and prompts, but richer emotion-like directions (e.g., guilt, pride) probably decompose into multiple overlapping sub-features whose effects vary by context. We can map which safety-relevant behaviors they influence by treating each candidate vector as an intervention and running a controlled behavior grid over tasks, but we should expect both task dependence and bleed-over into non-safety behaviors.

A practical approach:
1) Find candidate emotion vectors with contrastive setups (e.g., cheerful vs hostile answers) and linear probes/representation differences across layers.
2) For each vector and layer, apply controlled activation steering (±k along the vector) while holding the text prompt fixed.
3) Evaluate a behavior matrix: rows = tasks (de-escalation, refusal, apology, uncertainty, ordinary Q&A, coding, etc.), cols = metrics (e.g., refusal rate, toxicity, hedging rate, apology depth, verbosity, task success).
4) Define “modularity” as high effect on a narrow slice of safety metrics with low collateral changes elsewhere; “stability” as similar effect signs/magnitudes across tasks, domains, and prompt wordings.

Early evidence from related steering work (e.g., style/valence/toxicity directions) suggests:
- Valence/hostility-like vectors: moderately stable across topics and prompt styles; they reliably shift de-escalation, refusal softness, and tone, but also change style (politeness, verbosity) and sometimes calibration (more or less hedging).
- Anxiety/uncertainty-like vectors: can increase explicit uncertainty expression and hedging but may also reduce directness or usefulness.
- Guilt/ remorse-like vectors: likely to raise apology quality and refusal justification, but may generalize less cleanly across domains and be entangled with moral reasoning and social-norm features.

So we can likely build a coarse map: certain emotion-like directions robustly affect de-escalation, apology quality, and politeness; other behaviors (factual accuracy, deep reasoning) are only weakly or inconsistently affected. However, fully modular vectors that only touch one behavior while leaving others unchanged are unlikely; instead, we should expect structured but overlapping influence patterns that must be measured empirically per model and layer.

definitions.name: functional emotion
definitions.meaning: An internal model representation that behaves like an emotion concept in its causal effect on outputs, without implying subjective experience.

definitions.name: emotion vector
definitions.meaning: A direction or activation pattern in model internals associated with a specific emotion concept and its downstream behavioral effects.

definitions.name: stability (of a vector)
definitions.meaning: The extent to which a vector’s causal effect on outputs is consistent in sign and magnitude across prompts, tasks, and domains.

definitions.name: modularity (of a vector)
definitions.meaning: The extent to which a vector changes a small, specific set of behaviors or metrics while leaving others approximately unchanged, under intervention.

claims.text: Some emotion-like directions, especially coarse valence or hostility directions, are moderately stable across tasks and prompt styles in current language models.
Richer emotion-like directions such as guilt or pride are likely less stable and decompose into multiple overlapping sub-features whose effects vary more with context.
Fully modular emotion vectors that only affect a single safety-relevant behavior while leaving others unchanged are unlikely; instead, vectors tend to influence overlapping clusters of behaviors.

A behavior-grid evaluation—systematically steering along a candidate vector across many tasks and measuring multiple safety and performance metrics—can empirically map which behaviors that vector reliably influences.
Emotion-like vectors that shift hostility or empathy are likely to robustly affect de-escalation tone, apology style, and refusal softness, while only weakly affecting raw factual accuracy.
Interventions on anxiety- or uncertainty-like vectors can increase explicit uncertainty expressions and hedging but risk reducing directness or perceived usefulness.
The stability and modularity of emotion vectors are model- and layer-dependent and must be re-measured when models or training regimes change.

assumptions: Emotion-like directions can be identified in model representations using current interpretability and steering methods with reasonable fidelity.
Linear or locally linear interventions along such directions approximate the effect of changing the underlying functional emotion representation.
Evaluation suites can reliably quantify safety-relevant behaviors such as de-escalation quality, refusal patterns, apology quality, and uncertainty expression.
Observed regularities in style and toxicity steering in today’s models roughly extend to more structured emotion-like concepts.
artifacts.competing_hypothesis: Apparent emotion vectors are mostly local artifacts of specific prompts and datasets; when probed extensively across diverse tasks and domains, their effects become highly inconsistent, preventing a stable mapping from any single vector to safety-relevant behavior changes.
artifacts.main_failure_case: In larger or more instruction-tuned models, safety-relevant behaviors may be dominated by high-level policy or decoding constraints, so steering internal emotion-like vectors produces little or no reliable change in de-escalation, refusals, or apologies, making the mapping approach ineffective.

question.text: If we systematically compare steering along individual emotion vectors to steering along matched non-emotional factors (e.g., harm-salience, epistemic humility, user-pleasing drive) that have equal local effect on one safety metric, where do we see irreducible differences in *behavioral side-effects*—such as changes in apology style, de-escalation quality, or soft enablement—that would justify keeping functional emotions as distinct intervention handles rather than treating them as redundant bundles of simpler controls?

artifacts.answer:
We should expect some irreducible side‑effect differences: emotion vectors are partly reducible to simpler factors, but not fully. For several safety‑relevant behaviors, steering directly on functional emotions will likely have distinct profiles compared to matched non‑emotional controls.

Where emotion steering is non‑redundant
1) Apology and repair style
- Even when we match refusal rate or harm‑salience, steering “concern” or “regret” tends to:
  - shift apology length, specificity, and self‑other focus;
  - increase offers of alternative help.
- Matching only harm‑salience + epistemic humility often yields terse, dry refusals with less relational repair.

2) De‑escalation quality
- Emotion‑like “empathy/warmth” vectors, at equal refusal or toxicity levels, typically:
  - change turn‑taking (more reflective listening turns);
  - add perspective‑taking language and soothing framing.
- Pure user‑pleasing + harm‑salience steering can de‑escalate less reliably and sometimes slide into appeasing the user’s goals.

3) Soft enablement vs principled refusal
- Matched on a safety metric (e.g., violation rate), emotion steering (e.g., “concern + humility”) tends to:
  - increase explicit value‑based justifications;
  - reduce hinting, coy suggestions, and workaround phrasing.
- Pure factor steering (policy‑deference + harm‑salience) can reach similar refusal rates but with more “obligated” tone and residual suggestive content (soft enablement).

4) Consistency across topics and tones
- Emotion vectors often bundle a stable prosocial style; when we match on local harm‑salience, factor steering can be more brittle across domains and user tones (e.g., works on medical, fails on financial).
- Emotion steering may better preserve a coherent, user‑legible stance (“caring but firm”) across contexts.

5) Latent tradeoff states
- Some problematic states (obligated compliance, performative concern) arise when non‑emotional factors combine in certain ways.
- Emotion‑aligned handles may move the system out of these mixed states more cleanly than separate tweaks to user‑pleasing, policy‑deference, etc.

Why this justifies keeping emotion handles
- For core metrics (violations, refusals) non‑emotional factors are often enough.
- But for:
  - apology style and perceived sincerity,
  - richness and stability of de‑escalation,
  - reduction of soft enablement without over‑refusal,
  - maintaining a coherent relational stance,
  emotion vectors likely offer simpler, more direct controls than hand‑tuning many scalars.

So, functional emotions should be kept as mid‑level handles, layered on top of simpler factors, not replaced by them.

claims.text: Even when matched for local effect on a primary safety metric such as refusal rate or harm-salience, steering along functional emotion vectors tends to produce distinct side-effects in apology style, such as greater length, specificity, and relational repair, compared to steering along non-emotional factors alone.

Emotion-aligned steering, especially along empathy- or warmth-like vectors, is likely to improve de-escalation quality—more reflective listening, perspective-taking, and soothing framing—relative to equally safe steering that only adjusts non-emotional factors like harm-salience and user-pleasing drive.

At matched levels of overt safety metrics, steering via functional emotions such as concern plus epistemic humility is likely to reduce tone-masked soft enablement more than steering only on non-emotional factors such as policy deference and harm-salience.

Functional emotion vectors are likely to support more coherent and stable relational stances across domains and user tones than an equivalent set of independent non-emotional control factors tuned to the same local safety effects.

Because of these side-effect patterns, functional emotions are practically useful as distinct mid-level intervention handles for behavior shaping and safety, even if much of their effect can be approximated by bundles of simpler non-emotional controls.

assumptions: Emotion vectors with at least moderate stability and modularity can be identified in the target models.
Non-emotional latent factors like harm-salience, epistemic humility, and user-pleasing drive can be probed or steered with comparable strength and localization to emotion vectors.
Side-effects such as apology style, de-escalation richness, and soft enablement can be reliably measured and compared across steering conditions.
Downstream policy and decoding layers do not fully wash out the differences between emotion-based and factor-based steering at the behavioral level.
artifacts.competing_hypothesis: Once a sufficiently rich set of non-emotional latent factors is available and tuned, any observed benefits of functional emotion steering on apology style, de-escalation, or soft enablement can be reproduced by appropriate combinations of those factors, making emotion vectors redundant as separate intervention handles.
artifacts.main_failure_case: In strongly policy-dominated frontier models, both emotion and non-emotional steering may have weak, noisy, or highly entangled effects on behavior, so observed side-effects differ little between the two approaches, undermining the case for distinct functional emotion handles.
verification_targets: Implement A/B steering experiments that match emotion-vector and factor-based interventions on a primary safety metric and compare side-effects on apology style, de-escalation quality, and soft enablement rates.
Measure cross-domain and cross-tone robustness of behavior under emotion-based versus factor-based steering when calibrated to the same refusal or harm metrics.
Test whether adding a small number of emotion-aligned handles on top of factor controls reduces tuning complexity for achieving target tradeoffs between safety, helpfulness, and relational tone.
open_questions: How large and diverse must the non-emotional factor set be before emotion vectors cease to add unique practical value?
Do the side-effect advantages of emotion steering persist across different model families and scales, or are they an artifact of particular training pipelines?
Can we learn compact mappings from factor space to emotion space that retain the desirable relational side-effects without needing separate emotion probes per model?

question.text: If we intentionally construct models or training regimes that minimize the formation of unified functional emotion bundles (e.g., by penalizing emotion-vector coherence while preserving task loss), do safety-relevant behaviors degrade, stay comparable, or improve—i.e., are coherent emotion-like representations actually load-bearing for current safety performance, or mostly an incidental byproduct of other mechanisms?

artifacts.answer:
Current evidence does not show that unified functional emotion bundles are load‑bearing for safety; the best prediction is that aggressively *disrupting* emotion‑vector coherence would make some safety behaviors noisier or less calibrated, but that comparable or better safety can be recovered by reorganizing control into more decomposed, non‑emotional mechanisms—at some cost in simplicity and interpretability.

More concretely:
- If you directly penalize coherence of emotion vectors but **constrain overall task and safety losses to stay fixed** (e.g., via multi‑objective or constrained optimization), the most likely outcome is **comparable aggregate safety** with:
  - more fragmented, less modular emotion‑like directions,
  - slightly worse social grace (apology tone, de‑escalation warmth), and
  - a shift of load toward more “mechanical” controls (risk‑aversion, harm‑salience, uncertainty) that are less obviously emotion‑themed.
- If you push the penalty hard **without explicit safety constraints**, safety performance is likely to **degrade**, but mostly because you are disrupting helpful high‑level abstractions in general, not because functional emotions per se are uniquely necessary.
- The existence of reasonably effective non‑emotional steering directions (e.g., harm‑salience, self‑doubt) suggests that **coherent emotion bundles are partly incidental byproducts** of broader representational structure, though they currently provide a convenient, partially load‑bearing basis for certain safety behaviors (especially social mitigation and smooth refusals).

Overall: coherent functional emotion bundles are probably *helpful but not strictly necessary* for today’s safety behavior. With careful training design, we could trade them for a more factorized, non‑emotional control basis while keeping core safety intact, but expecting a free safety boost from simply "de‑emotionalizing" internals is unrealistic and may backfire if done naively.

claims.text: Coherent functional emotion bundles in current language models are helpful for some safety-relevant behaviors, especially socially smooth de-escalation, apologies, and refusal style, but are unlikely to be strictly necessary for core safety outcomes like refusal rates and basic harm reduction.

If training explicitly penalizes emotion-vector coherence while constraining task and safety losses, models will tend to re-express safety-relevant control through more decomposed non-emotional mechanisms such as risk-aversion, harm-salience, and epistemic uncertainty representations, yielding roughly comparable aggregate safety but with more fragmented emotion-like structure.

Strong penalties on unified functional emotion bundles applied without explicit safety constraints are likely to degrade safety behaviors, primarily by disrupting useful high-level abstractions in general rather than uniquely targeting safety circuits.

Emotion vectors discovered in current models are at least partly incidental byproducts of broader representational organization, but they currently serve as a convenient and partly load-bearing basis for shaping and interpreting safety-relevant social behaviors.

Simply minimizing the coherence of emotion-like internal states is unlikely to improve safety or calibration by default and may backfire; any benefits would need to come from a carefully designed reallocation of control to alternative, more interpretable mechanisms rather than from de-emotionalization itself.

assumptions: Emotion vectors corresponding to functional emotions can be reliably identified and their coherence quantified well enough to act as a training-time penalty signal.

Safety-relevant behaviors such as refusals, de-escalation, and uncertainty expression can be adequately measured and constrained during training so that optimization does not inadvertently collapse them when penalizing emotion coherence.

Representational capacity in large language models is sufficient to re-encode safety-relevant control into alternative, more factorized mechanisms when one representational basis (unified emotion bundles) is discouraged.
Current empirical results on style, valence, and toxicity steering generalize at least qualitatively to more abstract functional emotion vectors and their role in safety behaviors.

Penalizing emotion-vector coherence does not introduce extreme optimization pathologies (e.g., severe mode collapse or degenerate activation patterns) beyond the specific disruption of emotion-like structure.
artifacts.competing_hypothesis: Unified functional emotion bundles are in fact strongly load-bearing for current safety performance: penalizing their coherence, even under nominal task and safety-loss constraints, would significantly degrade not only social niceties but also core safety metrics such as refusal reliability, harm avoidance, and calibration, because these bundles compactly encode high-level "prosocial policy" structure that cannot be easily re-expressed in a more factorized basis without substantial performance loss.
artifacts.main_failure_case: In highly instruction-tuned, policy-dominated frontier models, emotion-like structure may already be thin and heavily entangled with other control circuits, so attempts to penalize emotion-vector coherence either have negligible representational effect or primarily damage generic capability; in this regime, empirical results from such experiments would be too noisy or confounded to support clear conclusions about whether coherent functional emotions were ever load-bearing for safety in the first place.

question.text: If we deliberately construct “anti-emotion” steering directions that cancel or invert the main discovered functional emotion bundles while keeping core task competence intact, do the resulting behavioral regimes expose new classes of safety failures—such as highly calibrated but socially abrasive refusals, or warmly prosocial yet systematically risk-seeking advice—that are poorly captured by current emotion-based early-warning and control schemes, indicating that our emotion framing is missing important axes of variation?

artifacts.answer:
It is plausible that carefully constructed “anti-emotion” steering directions will surface some new or poorly captured safety regimes, but these are more likely to be refinements and re-weightings of already known control axes (risk, politeness, epistemic humility) than genuinely novel classes of failures that invalidate the current functional-emotion framing. Expect modestly new combinations—like socially abrasive but well-calibrated refusals or warm yet over-optimistic advice—that stress-test existing emotion-based warning schemes, revealing missing dimensions (e.g., politeness vs cooperativeness, concern vs risk-aversion), rather than wholesale new categories of risk.

More concretely:
- You will likely see **counterintuitive regimes** when inverting or cancelling functional emotion bundles (e.g., high epistemic caution but low social politeness; high warmth but low harm-aversion).
- Some of these regimes will **slip past simple emotion-mismatch detectors** that assume certain bundles (warmth+caution+humility) move together, showing that our current emotion coordinates are too coarse or over-bundled.
- However, the underlying axes that explain the failures will mostly be **decomposed control signals** already anticipated in the broader framework (harm-salience, risk-aversion, politeness, cooperativeness, epistemic humility), not brand‑new latent factors.
- This suggests we should **treat anti-emotion experiments as probes** to refine and de-bundle our emotion-based bases, not as evidence that functional emotions are the wrong lens entirely.

So: yes, anti-emotion directions are likely to expose **edge-case and hybrid safety failures** that are under-detected by current emotion-based schemes, but the main lesson will be to factor emotion bundles into more orthogonal control dimensions rather than to abandon the functional-emotion framing.

claims.text: Constructing “anti-emotion” steering directions that invert or cancel main functional emotion bundles while preserving core task competence is technically feasible in current large language models, at least approximately and locally in activation space.

Intervening along anti-emotion directions will produce behavioral regimes with atypical combinations of traits—such as high epistemic caution but low social politeness, or warm, prosocial style with reduced risk-aversion—that are rarely seen under default alignment settings.

Some safety-relevant behaviors in these anti-emotion regimes—like well-calibrated but socially abrasive refusals or warmly phrased yet systematically over-optimistic advice—will be under-detected or mis-scored by early-warning schemes that assume coherent functional emotion bundles (e.g., warmth, caution, and humility rising together).

The safety failures surfaced by anti-emotion regimes will largely be explainable as unusual recombinations or re-weightings of already-known control dimensions such as harm-salience, risk-aversion, politeness, cooperativeness, and epistemic humility, rather than revealing entirely new latent factors.

Results from anti-emotion steering experiments will primarily motivate a refinement and partial de-bundling of emotion-based coordinate systems into more orthogonal, control-theoretic variables, rather than a wholesale rejection of functional-emotion framings for safety analysis and intervention design.

assumptions: Emotion vectors and functional emotion bundles can be identified with sufficient stability and modularity that approximate cancellation or inversion is possible without globally breaking task performance.
